did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep transfer learning with pre-trained convolutional neural networks (ConvNeXt-Tiny, EfficientNetV2-S, DenseNet121, MobileNetV3-Large) for the imbalanced multi-class classification of non-melanoma skin cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, and benign Nevus) using dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2019 dataset. It also employed Explainable AI (Grad-CAM) for interpretability.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the benchmarking of four deep transfer learning models (ConvNeXt-Tiny, EfficientNetV2-S, DenseNet121, MobileNetV3-Large) on the ISIC 2019 dataset. It details specific methodology including data preprocessing, Focal Loss integration, 5-fold Stratified Group-KFold cross-validation, and Grad-CAM implementation. The study reports new quantitative results (e.g., Balanced Accuracy of 76.98%) and describes the deployment of a Streamlit web application, which constitutes original research rather than a literature review.

title: Benchmarking deep transfer learning for imbalanced skin cancer classification: Integrating focal loss, explainable AI, and web deployment
brief_summary: This study benchmarks deep transfer learning models for the imbalanced multi-class classification of non-melanoma skin cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, and benign Nevus) using dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2019 dataset. The research evaluates four architectures—ConvNeXt-Tiny, EfficientNetV2-S, DenseNet121, and MobileNetV3-Large—integrated with Focal Loss to address class imbalance and Grad-CAM for explainability. Evaluation via 5-fold Stratified Group-KFold cross-validation revealed that ConvNeXt-Tiny achieved the highest Balanced Accuracy of 76.98% and Macro F1-Score of 0.7513, significantly outperforming traditional baselines. The optimal model was subsequently deployed as a real-time Streamlit web application to serve as an interpretable clinical decision-support system. The findings demonstrate that modern architectures combined with Focal Loss effectively mitigate algorithmic bias in highly skewed medical datasets.

lesion_types_studied.details: Minority malignant class in the imbalanced multi-class classification task. Total count: 628 images in the ISIC 2019 dataset subset used.
Pre-malignant minority class in the imbalanced multi-class classification task. Total count: 867 images in the ISIC 2019 dataset subset used.
Majority benign class in the imbalanced multi-class classification task. Total count: 12,875 images in the ISIC 2019 dataset subset used. Represents approximately 89.6% of the total images.

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic categories: Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC), Actinic Keratosis (AK), and benign Nevus (NV)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of non-melanoma skin cancer using dermoscopic images. The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels (SCC, AK, NV) and outputs class probabilities for each category. The task addresses severe class imbalance (89.6% benign Nevus vs. minority malignant/pre-malignant classes) using Focal Loss.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2019 challenge archive, a public dermoscopic image dataset used for multi-class classification of non-melanoma skin cancer. The study utilized a subset focusing on Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, and benign Nevus.

use.training: Used for training the deep transfer learning models (ConvNeXt-Tiny, EfficientNetV2-S, DenseNet121, MobileNetV3-Large) via 5-fold Stratified Group-KFold cross-validation on the 90% split.
use.validation: Used for validation during the 5-fold Stratified Group-KFold cross-validation process to select the best-performing model state.
use.testing: Used as a hold-out test set (10% of unique lesions) to evaluate the final model's performance and generate the confusion matrix.
use.other: Used for benchmarking and deploying a Streamlit web application for clinical decision support.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate model performance while maintaining class proportions and preventing data leakage by keeping patient data isolated across folds.
To measure the consistency and stability of the models' Balanced Accuracy across the 5-fold cross-validation splits.
To evaluate performance on imbalanced multi-class classification by computing the arithmetic mean of sensitivity across all classes.
To evaluate performance by calculating the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall independently for each class and averaging them, treating all classes equally.

statistical_methods_used.method: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (ROC-AUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the architectures' performance for imbalanced multi-class classification.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Performance comparisons were based on descriptive statistics (Balanced Accuracy, Macro F1-Score, Standard Deviation) from 5-fold Stratified Group-KFold cross-validation.

ISIC 2019.experiment: Hold-out Test Set Evaluation (Best Model: ConvNeXt-Tiny)

ConvNeXt-Tiny.note: The paper states the model successfully mitigated majority-class bias but does not provide specific counts for SCC and AK true/false positives/negatives in the text.

ISIC 2019.notes: ConvNeXt-Tiny emerged as the superior model, significantly outperforming traditional baselines. The integration of Focal Loss (gamma=2.0) allowed the model to maintain high sensitivity for minority classes (SCC and AK) despite severe class imbalance (NV ~89.6%). Grad-CAM heatmaps confirmed the model focused on pathological lesion borders.

architecture_used.main_model: Four architectures were benchmarked: ConvNeXt-Tiny, EfficientNetV2-S, DenseNet121, and MobileNetV3-Large. ConvNeXt-Tiny was identified as the superior model.
architecture_used.pretraining: All models were pre-trained on the ImageNet-1k dataset.

ConvNeXt-Tiny.description: Modernized CNN adopting macro-designs from Vision Transformers (ViT).

EfficientNetV2-S.description: Optimizes training speed and parameter efficiency.
key_features: Standard 3 × 3 convolutions in early stages to improve memory access
Each layer receives feature maps from all preceding layers as inputs
Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) blocks for channel-wise feature recalibration

training_details.data_augmentation: Horizontal/vertical flips, random rotations, color jittering, CoarseDropout (cutout)

preprocessing_techniques: Morphological Black-Hat filtering to isolate dark, hair-like artifacts
Telea inpainting algorithm to reconstruct underlying skin texture after hair removal
Resizing images to a uniform dimension of 224 × 224 pixels
Dynamic data augmentation during training including horizontal and vertical flips, random rotations, color jittering (brightness and contrast), and CoarseDropout (cutout)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied dynamically during training using the Albumentations library to increase data diversity.
Applied dynamically during training using the Albumentations library to increase data diversity.
Applied dynamically during training using the Albumentations library to adjust brightness and contrast.
Applied dynamically during training using the Albumentations library to force the network to learn robust, distributed feature representations.

conclusions: Modernized deep transfer learning architectures significantly outperform traditional CNN baselines for imbalanced non-melanoma skin cancer classification.

ConvNeXt-Tiny emerged as the superior model, achieving a Balanced Accuracy of 76.98% and a Macro F1-Score of 0.7513, with the highest stability (standard deviation ± 0.31) among the tested architectures.

The integration of Focal Loss (gamma=2.0) successfully mitigated algorithmic bias caused by severe class imbalance, allowing the model to maintain high sensitivity for minority classes (SCC and AK) despite the majority class (NV) comprising approximately 89.6% of the data.

Grad-CAM explainable AI provided clinical transparency by confirming that the model focused on pathological lesion borders and ignored irrelevant background artifacts, aligning with pathological criteria.
Traditional lightweight models like MobileNetV3-Large struggled significantly (BAcc 47.04%) due to limited representational capacity, leading to overfitting on the majority benign class.
The optimal ConvNeXt-Tiny model was successfully deployed as a real-time Streamlit web application, establishing a practical, interpretable clinical decision-support system.
Future research should focus on integrating multimodal data, such as patient metadata, to further enhance diagnostic sensitivity and global generalizability.
important_remarks: The study is limited to non-melanoma skin cancer (SCC, AK, NV) using the ISIC 2019 dataset; it does not address malignant melanoma, limiting generalizability to broader skin cancer screening.
The dataset exhibits severe class imbalance (89.6% benign Nevus), which, while addressed via Focal Loss, may not reflect balanced clinical prevalence rates.
External validation is absent; the model was only evaluated on the ISIC 2019 dataset, raising concerns about generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
Data leakage risks were mitigated by using lesion_id for group-aware splitting, ensuring patient data isolation across folds, which strengthens the validity of the cross-validation results.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) to confirm the significance of performance differences between models.

Traditional lightweight models (MobileNetV3-Large) showed poor performance (BAcc 47.04%), indicating that computational efficiency trade-offs may compromise diagnostic accuracy in imbalanced settings.
The web deployment is a prototype using Streamlit; clinical utility and safety have not been validated through prospective clinical trials or user studies with dermatologists.

dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized the ISIC 2019 dataset, focusing on a subset of three classes: Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC), Actinic Keratosis (AK), and benign Nevus (NV). The dataset was split into a training/validation pool (90% of unique lesions) and a hold-out test set (10% of unique lesions). The 90% pool was used for 5-fold Stratified Group-KFold cross-validation to train and select the best model states. The hold-out test set was used for final performance evaluation. No external or cross-dataset validation was performed.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The study did not perform cross-dataset validation. All experiments were conducted on the ISIC 2019 dataset. The paper explicitly notes the absence of external validation as a limitation regarding generalizability.

training.notes: Used for training ConvNeXt-Tiny, EfficientNetV2-S, DenseNet121, and MobileNetV3-Large via 5-fold Stratified Group-KFold cross-validation. The 90% pool of unique lesions was used for this purpose.

validation.notes: Used as the validation split within the 5-fold Stratified Group-KFold cross-validation process to select the best-performing model state based on Balanced Accuracy.

testing.notes: Used as a hold-out test set (10% of unique lesions) to evaluate the final selected model's performance and generate the confusion matrix.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: Used to provide pre-trained weights for the deep transfer learning architectures (ConvNeXt-Tiny, EfficientNetV2-S, DenseNet121, MobileNetV3-Large). Images were normalized using ImageNet-1k statistics.

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: Dynamic data augmentation was applied during training using the Albumentations library, including horizontal/vertical flips, random rotations, color jittering, and CoarseDropout. No synthetic datasets were generated.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: 90% of unique lesions used for cross-validation (training/validation), 10% of unique lesions reserved as a hold-out test set. Split was based on lesion_id to prevent data leakage.
evaluation_protocol.cross_validation_details: 5-fold Stratified Group-KFold cross-validation. Folds were stratified by class and grouped by lesion_id to ensure patient/lesion data isolation across folds.
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: No unseen external dataset was used. The hold-out test set was part of the ISIC 2019 dataset.

dataset_level_results.notes: Results are averaged over 5-fold Stratified Group-KFold cross-validation. ConvNeXt-Tiny outperformed EfficientNetV2-S (BAcc 76.28%), DenseNet121 (BAcc 55.85%), and MobileNetV3-Large (BAcc 47.04%).

other_metrics.Confusion Matrix Counts: 1,270 out of 1,311 benign NV cases correctly identified. Specific counts for SCC and AK not explicitly reported in text.

dataset_level_results.notes: Evaluation on the unseen hold-out test set (1,447 images). The model successfully mitigated majority-class bias.

generalizability_notes: The study is limited to non-melanoma skin cancer (SCC, AK, NV) using the ISIC 2019 dataset; it does not address malignant melanoma, limiting generalizability to broader skin cancer screening.
External validation is absent; the model was only evaluated on the ISIC 2019 dataset, raising concerns about generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
The dataset exhibits severe class imbalance (89.6% benign Nevus), which may not reflect balanced clinical prevalence rates.
Data leakage risks were mitigated by using lesion_id for group-aware splitting, ensuring patient data isolation across folds.
information_not_reported: No external test set results were reported.
No p-values or formal statistical hypothesis testing were reported for performance differences between models.
Specific confusion matrix counts for minority classes (SCC, AK) on the hold-out test set were not explicitly provided in the text.
